preamble: The alveolar nasal consonant [n] can undergo a phonetic transformation into the close front rounded vowel [y] through a process of vocalization and lenition. This shift is typically conditioned by specific phonological environments.

examples.note: In Spokane Salish, an 'n-shift' rule dictates that /n/ vocalizes to [y] when it occurs after a vowel and before a following sibilant /s/ in certain morphological contexts.

sources: Kinkade, M. D. (1976). The n Shift in Spokane Salish. International Journal of American Linguistics, 42(2), 133-139. The University of Chicago Press.
